articles.title: When KYC Becomes a Barrier: Supreme Court’s Stand for Digital Inclusion
articles.summary: In its April 30, 2025 judgment, the Supreme Court recognised the exclusion caused by E-KYC systems and held that digital access is a fundamental right under Article 21 of the Indian Constitution.
articles.content: Donate Now tl;dr In a judgement dated April 30, 2025 delivered in the case of Pragya Prasun & Ors. vs. UOI & Ors. , W.P.(C) No. 289 of 2024 along with Amar Jain vs. UOI & Ors ., W.P.(C) No. 45 of 2025 the Supreme Court of India observed that the right to digital access is an intrinsic component of the right to life and liberty. This ruling and its accompanying observations serve as a strong foundation for future petitions advocating for greater digital inclusion. Why should you care? Digital technology has undoubtedly made many aspects of life easier but when systems rely solely on it, without offering alternatives, they can end up excluding the very people they are meant to serve. For many citizens, especially persons with disabilities and those from marginalised communities, the push for digital KYC has become a barrier rather than a bridge. But exclusion is not limited to the vulnerable and marginalized alone. Even educated and otherwise eligible individuals can be denied opportunities due to technical glitches. People are being denied basic services because of things as simple as a fingerprint mismatch, a technical error, or not having access to a smartphone or stable internet. This is not just a technical issue—it is a human one. A system that does not account for the needs of all kinds of people, including persons with disabilities, is not an inclusive system. Our Constitution promises equality to every citizen under Article 14. When policies leave out entire sections of society, especially the most vulnerable, it becomes essential to call that out and seek accountability. The Supreme Court’s recent judgment is a powerful reminder that digital access is not just a policy goal—it is part of our fundamental rights, and it must be protected as such. Background On 30th April, the Supreme Court in the case of Pragya Prasun & Ors. vs. UOI & Ors., W.P.(C) No. 289 of 2024 along with Amar Jain vs. UOI & Ors., W.P.(C) No. 45 of 2025 issued 20 directions aimed at ensuring that e-KYC and other digital public services are made “universally accessible.” In doing so, the Court underscored that digital inclusion is not merely a matter of convenience, but a constitutional imperative and declared digital access to be an intrinsic component to Right to Life and Liberty. This decision stemmed from two petitions filed by acid attack survivors, led by Pragya Prasun, and Amar Jain, a visually impaired lawyer. The petitions highlighted the challenges they face in accessing digital services, especially those that rely on facial recognition or eye movement for verification. The petitioners described how they are routinely excluded from essential services, as digital KYC processes frequently require actions such as facial movements or blinking—tasks that are not possible for many individuals with facial disfigurements or visual impairments. Financial institutions are required by law to conduct KYC (Know Your Customer) procedures to verify the identity and other details of their clients to crack down on fraudulent transactions. Aadhaar-based e-KYC enables the identity and address of a customer to be verified electronically via Aadhaar authentication, making the entire process paperless. The customer provides their explicit consent (by providing biometrics or OTP) for the e-KYC service to instantly generate an electronic, non-repudiable proof of identity and proof of address. This case is just one example where the process of authentication of identity verification has been digitised and the people have been excluded from the process. This reality also extends to various other marginalized populations who are getting excluded on a daily basis due to biometric mismatches and other Aadhaar based identity systems, which have been made mandatory. In this context, the judgment of the Supreme Court underscores the importance of digital access in today’s world and provides a solid basis for future petitions seeking to advance digital inclusion. Digital Identity Exclusion: The Persistent Problem The problem of digital identity exclusion has two key dimensions: one stems from authentication failures, while the other arises from the risk of being entirely removed from official records. In recent years, Aadhaar-based identity systems have been increasingly implemented by both Union and State government agencies across India to authenticate individuals for access to a wide range of public services. These systems are now widely used in public offices, hospitals, and schools. However, their deployment often lacks sufficient oversight and safeguards, posing significant risks to citizens’ sensitive data and potentially excluding those who lack access to or familiarity with digital technology. The debate regarding exclusion from access to services is not unique to e-KYC and has been raised several times, especially with regard to Aadhaar-based and biometric-linked identity systems. The aim of these systems is to verify the identity and address of an individual electronically, making the entire process paperless. The process commences with the customer providing their explicit consent (by providing biometrics or OTP) for the e-KYC service to instantly generate an electronic, non-repudiable proof of identity and proof of address. The government asserts that the digitalisation aims to enhance transparency, efficiency and saves time while safeguarding the rights of citizens. However, this has not been the reality. In the Comptroller and Auditor General of India (CAG) report on the functioning of UIDAI, 2021, it was acknowledged that the need for biometric update could arise on account of authentication failures due to incorrect biometric capture or poor biometric quality captured at the time of enrollment. Excerpt from the CAG report highlighting the problem of authentication failures Excerpt from the CAG report with recommendations Major instances of exclusion caused by technology have primarily resulted from fundamental issues in the authentication process. Recently, thousands of teenage ration card holders in Tamil Nadu were at risk of losing access to subsidised food grains under the National Food Security Act (NFSA) as outdated Aadhaar biometrics recorded years ago have stalled their e-KYC verification. Most of the affected teenagers are school students aged 13 to 17 from economically disadvantaged families. Their Aadhaar biometrics were recorded five to six years ago when they were under the age of 10, and the system now fails to recognise their fingerprints, official sources said. Similarly, beneficiaries above the age of 70 are facing verification issues due to faded fingerprints. Additionally, the e-KYC process has contributed to the exclusion of ration cardholders, especially among migrants. These concerns are not new, but they take on renewed urgency in light of the recent mandate requiring e-KYC updates. Individuals who fail to update their information risk being excluded from benefits under the Public Distribution System (PDS). As a result, many states are struggling to manage the e-KYC update process, even as millions have already been excluded from the food security net. This exclusion stems from the government's continued reliance on outdated data, largely due to delays in conducting the national census as well. In recent years, numerous reports have also highlighted cases of workers being wrongly marked as deceased in the Management Information System (MIS) of the Mahatma Gandhi National Rural Employment Guarantee Act, 2005 (NREGA). In reality, these individuals are very much alive but remain unaware that they have been removed from the system, a situation affecting many workers who were previously listed on the MGNREGS rolls without their knowledge. On January 01, 2024, the Union Rural Development Ministry made the use of Aadhaar-Based Payment System (“ABPS”) mandatory for the payment of wages to rural workers under the NREGA, and announced that non-compliance will be met with penalties. Further, the NREGA Mobile Monitoring System (“NMMS”) App which was made mandatory for workers to mark their attendance last year, continues to be inaccessible and inaccurate, costing them their daily wage. It has been pointed out repeatedly that as ABPS was made mandatory, NREGA account deletions shot up. In the 2024 Winter Session of Parliament, Congress MP K.C. Venugopal asked a question stating that 10.43 crore job card deletions had taken place under the NREGA scheme in the last four years across the country and sought to know whether it was because of the introduction of the Aadhaar-based payment system that became mandatory on January 1, 2024. According to data provided by LibTech India, a consortium of academics and activists, between April and September 2024, 84.8 lakh workers’ names were deleted from the scheme. Last year, LibTech data showed that 8 crore people were removed from the MGNREGA registry during the financial years 2022-23 and 2023-24. The true tragedy of these deletions lies not only in the loss of livelihood for millions of rural workers but also in the systemic failure to uphold the core principles of NREGA, a program intended to serve as a safety net for the most vulnerable members of society. In response to these allegations, the Union Minster replied by stating that the union government has no role in the deletion of job cards under the NREGA scheme and its responsibility lies with the state governments. Although state governments execute the deletions at the ground level, the Union government shares equal responsibility for the crisis due to its policy decisions, outdated guidelines, and failure to address underlying systemic problems. The Internet Freedom Foundation (IFF) , along with several other organisations and campaigns—most notably Rethink Aadhaar—has consistently raised concerns and written to various government departments about the risks of exclusion and privacy violations arising from the mandatory Aadhaar linkage for access to State welfare schemes. At IFF’s annual event, Privacy Supreme 2024 , held on August 22, 2024, Nikhil Dey, Founding Member of the Mazdoor Kisan Shakti Sangathan, shed light on the ground realities of the Aadhaar project. Through firsthand accounts, he highlighted how individuals have been excluded from pension rolls and other social security schemes, underscoring the harmful consequences of so-called “voluntary mandatory” schemes, particularly for rural communities. Therefore, implementing technological interventions in social security and welfare delivery—without first addressing the deep digital divide and low levels of internet literacy in the country—is a policy decision that inevitably leads to exclusion and infringes on the fundamental rights of countless individuals. Digital Divide The judgment also draws attention to the deep-rooted digital divide in India. The Court observed that individuals in rural and remote areas face poor internet connectivity, limited digital literacy, and a lack of regional language content—barriers that prevent effective access to e-governance and welfare services. Excerpt from the judgment in WP(C) No. 289 of 2024 & WP(C) No. 49 of 2025 The India Inequality Report 2022 highlights that 70% of the population either lacks or has inadequate access to digital services. Flagship initiatives like BharatNet, intended to expand rural connectivity, have fallen short of their targets. Among the poorest 20% of households, a mere 2.7% have access to a computer, and only 8.9% have internet connectivity. The report further reveals that only 38% of Indian households are digitally literate, with internet usage in rural areas standing at just 31%, compared to 67% in urban areas. Complementing this, data from the Telecom Regulatory Authority of India shows stagnation in wireless broadband subscriber growth. The trend highlights that a significant portion of the population still lacks the digital infrastructure needed for processes like e-KYC. Graph showing the number of subscribers every 4 months from 2020 to January 2025 The graph depicting wireless broadband subscribers in India every quarter from January 2020 to January 2025 shows a steady rise from approximately 650 million in January 2020 to just under 900 million by January 2025. A sharp increase is visible in the first half of 2020, with subscriber numbers jumping by nearly 50 million between January and July 2020—likely reflecting the urgent shift to digital infrastructure during the early pandemic. However, post-2021, growth becomes sluggish and uneven. Notably, between mid-2023 and mid-2024, subscriber numbers plateaued around 880 to 900 million, showing no substantial growth for nearly a year. This stagnation is not merely a statistical anomaly—it signals a deeper systemic issue: the calcification of digital divide. As access to wireless internet becomes critical for accessing education, welfare schemes, and employment, the lack of continued growth in wireless subscription suggests that large sections of the population are unable to cross the digital threshold. Key Takeaways from the Judgment The judgment authored by Justice Mahadevan clearly holds that the right to digital access is an essential part of the right to life and liberty under Article 21 of the Constitution. In today’s digital age, access to services like education, healthcare, banking, and welfare depends heavily on digital platforms. Yet, persons with disabilities, rural communities, senior citizens, women, and linguistic minorities continue to face serious exclusion due to inaccessible design, lack of digital literacy, poor connectivity, and limited regional language support. Excerpt from the judgment in WP(C) No. 289 of 2024 & WP(C) No. 49 of 2025 The Court specifically addressed the barriers in digital KYC systems, noting that visual tasks like facial recognition, CAPTCHA, or aligning one’s face are impossible for persons with visual impairments or facial disfigurements. As a result, they are often denied basic services. The Court affirmed that such exclusion violates both constitutional rights and disability laws like the RPwD Act, 2016 and the UNCRPD, and called for digital platforms—government and private—to comply with accessibility standards like the Web Content Accessibility Guidelines (WCAG). Excerpt from the judgment in WP(C) No. 289 of 2024 & WP(C) No. 49 of 2025 Importantly, the Court recognized the petitioners’ right to demand reasonable accommodations and emphasized that digital KYC guidelines must be revised with accessibility at their core. In doing so, it issued 20 specific directions to ensure digital KYC is accessible to persons with disabilities, especially those with visual impairments or facial/eye disfigurements due to acid attacks. Excerpt from the judgment in WP(C) No. 289 of 2024 & W P (C ) No. 49 of 2025 This judgment is more than a legal win—it is a powerful affirmation that digital systems must serve everyone, not just the tech-savvy or able-bodied. As the India Inequality Report 2022 shows, digital gaps are also gendered—only 31% of women own phones compared to 61% of men. In a country where one failed fingerprint scan can cost someone their ration or wage, the Court’s recognition of digital access as a fundamental right is a step toward real, inclusive justice—but the true challenge lies in implementation. Even before this landmark judgment, various Courts had recognized the problems caused by biometric authentication failures and digital exclusion. But this is the first time the Supreme Court has directly addressed the issue from the perspective of persons with disabilities. For instance, the Madhya Pradesh High Court, in a judgment dated March 25, 2025, held that a person’s identity cannot be determined solely by a machine and that biometric failure cannot be grounds to deny legal rights. Courts across the country are now speaking in one voice: alternatives to biometric authentication must be provided. Excerpt from the judgment in W P No. 8990 of 2020 and WP No. 6148 of 2020 Such alternative systems are not just legal safeguards—they are lifelines for people excluded due to fingerprint mismatches, poor internet access, disabilities, or lack of digital literacy. The right to access public services should not depend on one’s ability to operate a smartphone or align their face within a frame—it should depend only on their identity as a citizen. Even in Justice K.S. Puttaswamy (Retd.) v. Union of India , (2019) 1 SCC 1, where the Supreme Court upheld the constitutionality of the Aadhaar Act, it clearly recognized that exclusion from welfare benefits due to authentication failure is unacceptable. The Court noted that while the Aadhaar system aims to reduce leakages and ensure targeted delivery, it cannot come at the cost of denying benefits to those who genuinely qualify. It stressed that individuals who are unable to authenticate due to fingerprint mismatch, iris scan failure, or disability must be given alternate means to establish their identity. The judgment explicitly called for "suitable provisions" to prevent exclusion caused by technological limitations. Government needs to do more As the government intensifies the efforts in the digitisation of public services, it must also invest in strengthening offline support systems to ensure no one is excluded in the process. Common Service Centres (CSCs), set up under the Digital India initiative, are vital in bridging this gap. Located in rural and remote areas, CSCs enable citizens—especially those lacking internet access or digital literacy—to access essential services like ID registration, utility payments, and official certificates without having to travel to government offices. To truly make digital systems inclusive, CSCs must be equipped to provide alternative enrollment options and conduct outreach programs that build digital awareness, ensuring that the transition to a digital-first model leaves no one behind. This point becomes even more critical in the backdrop of the government's flagship scheme, the Pradhan Mantri Gramin Digital Saksharta Abhiyan (“PMGDISHA”). Launched in 2017, PMGDISHA aimed to provide basic digital literacy to 60 million citizens across rural India within two years. Between 2019 and 2022, the Pradhan Mantri Gramin Digital Saksharta Abhiyan (PMGDISHA) received consistent funding, with allocations of ₹400 crore in 2019–20, ₹300 crore in 2020–21, and ₹250 crore in both 2021–22 and 2022–23. However, from 2023–24 onwards, no funds have been allocated to the scheme, despite having not fully achieved its stated objectives. The discontinuation of such a vital programme, intended to empower the country's most digitally underserved communities, is deeply concerning. PMGDISHA played a key role in enabling rural citizens to access essential government, healthcare, and financial services online. Its absence leaves a significant gap in India’s push toward inclusive digital transformation. Another pressing issue the government must address is the lack of effective grievance redressal mechanisms within current systems. The Unique Identification Authority of India (“UIDAI”), set up a grievance redressal mechanism centrally to receive grievances through different channels which are highlighted below. Excerpt from the CAG report highlighting the Grievance Redressal Mechanism The CAG report highlighted a critical flaw in the existing grievance redressal mechanism: the inordinate delay in resolving complaints. One key issue is that grievances raised at the Regional Office level often fail to reach the attention of UIDAI headquarters, significantly undermining the system’s effectiveness. Additionally, the current grievance redressal process appears to be designed with the assumption that users have access to mobile phones—an assumption that excludes large sections of the population, particularly in rural and marginalized communities. This disconnect forces many citizens to run from pillar to post in search of resolution for issues that directly impact their access to essential services and rightful entitlements. What is urgently needed is a grievance redressal system underpinned by procedural safeguards, similar to those established in the Right to Information Act, 2005. Under RTI, there are clear timelines—such as a 30-day limit for the Public Information Officer to respond—followed by an appeals process that escalates to higher authorities, including the Central Information Commission. At every level, officials must provide a written, reasoned decision under their name, thereby ensuring transparency and accountability. In contrast, the current Aadhaar-linked grievance system lacks both responsiveness and accessibility. This not only erodes trust in public institutions but disproportionately harms the most vulnerable, who may not possess the digital access or bureaucratic literacy needed to navigate such systems. A rights-based, transparent, and inclusive grievance mechanism is not merely a technical fix—it is essential to safeguarding dignity, equity, and trust in public service delivery. Therefore, it is constitutionally imperative for the government to design systems that are not exclusively reliant on biometric or digital authentication. In light of the recent precedents, re-affirming digital access as part of the Right to Life and Liberty should compel policymakers to integrate inclusive alternatives into all digital public service systems—because a truly just and efficient welfare system does not punish citizens for the limitations of the very tools designed to help them. Important Documents: Pragya Prasun & Ors. vs. UOI & Ors., W.P.(C) No. 289 of 2024 along with Amar Jain vs. UOI & Ors., W.P.(C) No. 45 of 2025 ( Link ) Vinod Kumar Meena vs. Life Insurance Corporation of India, W.P. No. 8990 of 2020 ( Link )

articles.title: A RTI based investigation of Misinformation Control and Fact-Check Units
articles.summary: This post is the Part 1 of our investigation around fact check unites based on RTIs filed with states and Union Territories. The responses reveal distinct models of fact checking that give rise to concerns for free expression.
articles.content: Donate Now tl;dr State governments are setting up, or claiming to have constituted fact-check units to tackle online misinformation. Through Right to Information requests to all States and Union Territories starting in July, 2024 we sought details on their measures. The responses reveal concerns including — vague procedures, risk of political bias, and lack of independent oversight — raising the threat of arbitrary censorship and erosion of free speech. In this blogpost, which forms the first part of a three part series, we will analyse one of the most common responses we received – criminalisation of spreading misinformation and subsequent involvement of law enforcement agencies. Why should you care? The way misinformation is tackled directly affects everyone as more people rely on online platforms to keep updated. Here, both misinformation and the censorship of speech impacts our fundamental rights to receive information and in turn free speech. When governments and law enforcement agencies are given broad, unchecked powers to monitor and remove online content without clear safeguards, it opens the door to arbitrary censorship, political bias, and suppression of dissent. Hence, without strong protections, systems meant to fight misinformation can easily be misused to silence criticism, stifle public debate, and weaken democratic accountability. Protecting freedom of expression, ensuring transparency in governance, and demanding fair and independent mechanisms are essential not just for activists or journalists, but for every citizen who values an open and democratic society. Background After the brutal terrorist attack in Pahalgam on April 22, 2025 we have witnessed rampant misinformation spike online. Social media platforms have become battlegrounds where false narratives, doctored videos, and unverified claims are spreading at an alarming rate, often faster than official updates can keep up. Fake footage of military strikes, misattributed images of destruction, and fabricated government announcements are being widely circulated to inflame public sentiment, rally nationalist fervour, or discredit the opposing side. At the same time there has been illegal censorship which has been implemented in violation of any principles of natural justice. In this context, it becomes especially important to understand how governments are currently responding to misinformation. At this point many have stated the need for the government to fact check more proactively. However, an expansion of any censorial power which accompanies it has already been attempted and struck down as unconstitutional. A short refresher on it may be useful at this point. The Information Technology (Intermediary Guidelines and Digital Media Ethics Code) Amendment Rules, 2023 (" IT Rules, 2023 ") empowered the Central Government to establish a Fact-Check Unit (“ FCU ”) with broad powers to evaluate online content and enforce compliance. The terms "fake," "false," or "misleading" information, along with the vague concept of the "business of the Central Government," granted the FCU extensive discretion in determining what constitutes misinformation about government affairs. On September 26, 2024, in a landmark ruling , the Bombay High Court struck down this provision, declaring it unconstitutional (read more here ). Justice Chandurkar (the third judge), in his judgement , disagreed with the Central Government's argument that the right to freedom of speech under Article 19(1)(a) of the Indian Constitution included a "right to the truth," which would require the State to ensure the dissemination of only accurate information. He found that the IT Rules, 2023 violated Article 19(2) by imposing restrictions beyond its permissible scope, arbitrarily distinguished between digital and print media (violating Articles 19(1)(g) and 14), and vested unchecked power in the government without clear standards, creating a chilling effect on free speech. Furthermore, the IT Rules, 2023, introduced substantive obligations that extended beyond the mandate of the Information Technology Act (“ IT Act ”), 2000, especially Sections 69A and 79, and lacked necessary safeguards against misuse. Despite this ruling, numerous state governments have moved ahead, setting up their own FCUs (or similar bodies) to purportedly address misinformation online. A RTI based study of state level FCUs To better understand these evolving state-level frameworks, we filed two sets of Right to Information (“ RTI ”) requests with the Home Departments of all states and Union Territories in July 2024, followed by two additional sets in January 2025. The first (generic) set of RTIs focused on measures adopted to monitor and control fake, false, and misleading information, while the second (specific) sought detailed information regarding the legal authority, structure, composition, powers, and standard operating procedures of any established FCUs or similar bodies. It is important to note that we were unable to file RTIs with Rajasthan due to procedural barriers, including the mandatory requirement of an Aadhaar ID and a Google account to access their online RTI portal. The responses were systematically reviewed and analysed to assess the operational frameworks and practices of each state. A clear pattern emerged: either there was the involvement of law enforcement agencies and the criminalisation of misinformation, or the constitution of FCUs, which may also lead to criminalisation, alongside states citing confidentiality and claiming exemption under the RTI Act. Kerala 09.01.2025 Link 15.01.2025 Link 18.07.2025 Returned 23.07.2024 Returned This is the first in a three-part series on how states are tackling online misinformation. This first part explores the role of law enforcement agencies in addressing misinformation, the second part will delve into the constitution of FCUs, and the third part will examine states that have claimed exemptions under the RTI Act. Reliance on Law Enforcement Agencies Across Indian states, law enforcement agencies are playing an increasingly central role in identifying and responding to online misinformation, often through the criminalisation of spreading false information under criminal laws and the IT Act, 2000. While this reflects a heightened concern over the harms of misleading information, the strategies adopted by states differ widely in terms of scope, intensity, legal grounding, and transparency. A review of state-level RTI responses reveals significant variation in how law enforcement agencies monitor, act upon, and regulate misinformation, highlighting the diverse approaches employed across the country. 1. Monitoring Mechanisms: Proactive Surveillance versus Complaint-Based Action Several states have established proactive mechanisms to monitor misinformation. In Arunachal Pradesh and Mizoram , cyber crime units actively scan social media platforms. Similarly, Maharashtra and the Andaman and Nicobar Islands (Cyber Police) assign their cyber police teams to conduct real-time monitoring and intelligence gathering around potentially harmful or misleading content. Different districts in Jammu and Kashmir operate either Social Media Monitoring Cells, Electronic Surveillance Units (“ ESU ”), or Cyber Crime Cells, reflecting a more institutionalised surveillance structure. Conversely, in states like Karnataka and Tripura, misinformation monitoring is largely reactive. In a few districts of Karnataka ( Kempegowda , VV Puram , Shankarapura , CKA ) district police teams are tasked with this role. While in other districts ( Girinagar and KS Layout ), the aggrieved person may approach the police station and appropriate action under Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (“ BNS ”) and IT Act, 2000 will be taken as per the law. If the news is disturbing public order then suo-moto action may be taken. In Tripura , while social media is monitored, some districts act only upon receipt of public complaints. Chandigarh (responses from DSP Cyber Crime to specific RTI, 2024 and DSP Cyber Crime to generic and specific RTIs, 2025) follows a similar approach, where complaints submitted to the Social Media Team may lead to action, but only after legal vetting and approval by competent authorities. 2. Takedown Practices and Legal Authority States vary considerably in their capacity and authority to act on content identified as harmful. Punjab (responses from Cyber Crime Cell to generic RTI, 2024 and Cyber Crime Cell to generic and specific RTIs, 2025) invokes Sections 69A and 79(3)(b) of the Information Technology Act, 2000, to initiate takedowns or legal proceedings. Maharashtra (Cyber Police) also invokes these provisions, in conjunction with the BNS, 2023, and coordinates with the Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology (“ MeitY ”) and CERT-In for content removal under the Information Technology (Intermediary Guidelines and Digital Media Ethics Code) Rules, 2021 (“ IT Rules, 2021 ”). In Delhi , the police are designated as the nodal agency under Section 79(3)(b) of the IT Act, 2000, as outlined in the IT Rules, 2021. Content removal is overseen by the Joint Commissioner of Police. However, as with other states, there are no publicly available criteria outlining what constitutes "harmful" or "misleading" content, raising concerns about subjectivity in enforcement. Copy of Delhi Gazette dated December 26, 2024 The Andaman and Nicobar Islands represent a more extreme case. Here, the CID not only removes and prosecutes content under Section 79(3)(b), but they also resort to internet shutdowns in cases where misinformation is perceived to incite violence or disorder. While individuals may seek judicial redress if their content is removed, the discretionary power exercised by authorities remains largely unchecked. Law enforcement in these islands also collaborates closely with internet service providers, social media companies, and central cyber crime units for monitoring and enforcement. Screenshot of response from Superintendent of Police, CID, Andaman and Nicobar dated September 14, 2024 to the generic RTI filed in 2024 Chandigarh (responses from DSP Cyber Crime to specific RTI, 2024 and DSP Cyber Crime to generic and specific RTIs, 2025) requires written complaints before initiating takedown procedures, and only proceed once a competent authority grants permission. Interestingly, in Arunachal Pradesh , where 55 social media profiles were removed by the cyber crime unit via the Law Enforcement Online Request System (“ LEORS ”), authorities refused to disclose the rationale or case details, citing exemptions under Section 8(1)(h) of the RTI Act, 2005. This lack of transparency impairs public trust and impedes oversight. Similarly, one of the districts in Haryana – Faridabad claims to issue takedown notices for fake or misleading content but maintains no official records of these actions. Our Concerns The increasing reliance on law enforcement agencies and the criminalisation of misinformation raise significant concerns about the balance between safeguarding public order and protecting citizens' rights to free speech. While curbing harmful content is a legitimate concern, the lack of clear, transparent guidelines for identifying and managing misinformation leaves room for potential abuse of power. The broad discretion granted to law enforcement—whether at the district level in Karnataka or through Delhi Police—raises the risk of arbitrary enforcement. This could lead to selective action, where certain viewpoints or groups are disproportionately targeted, especially when definitions of "fake" or "misleading" content are vague and open to interpretation. States like Arunachal Pradesh and Mizoram, where cyber crime units actively monitor social media, and Tripura, which relies on public complaints, exemplify how the lack of specific criteria can lead to biased or politically motivated action, suppressing dissenting voices or minority opinions. In more extreme cases, such as the internet shutdowns in the Andaman and Nicobar Islands, law enforcement overreach in the name of combating misinformation disrupts citizens' access to information and stifles public discourse. This limits the free flow of ideas, further compounding risks to civil liberties. The threats posed by criminal prosecutions The responses to our RTIs also reflect the growing use of the BNS, 2023, as a formalised tool for criminalising digital speech. Provisions such as Section 353, which targets the circulation of false information likely to cause public alarm, and Section 197(1)(d), which addresses actions prejudicial to national integration, have been invoked by several states alongside the IT Act, 2000. These provisions prescribe significant penalties, underscoring the shift towards punitive state responses. The vague language in these laws—such as references to "public tranquillity" or "prejudice to national integrity"—further invites subjective interpretation and potential misuse. Section 69A of the IT Act, 2000, which empowers the government to block online content, also ties non-compliance with blocking orders to criminal penalties, including imprisonment of up to seven years. In this context, the Madras High Court's judgment in A. Kamala v. The State and Others offers a crucial counterpoint to the criminalisation trend. Responding to the detention of an individual under the Tamil Nadu Goondas Act, 1982, for allegedly circulating false information about a tender process, the Court asserted that not all instances of misinformation justify criminal prosecution—especially when there is no immediate threat to public order. The Court further underscored the need for preventive detention to be grounded in clear, tangible evidence, warning against the misuse of "public order" as a justification for suppressing dissent. The Court's emphasis on protecting social media expressions, even if critical or unpleasant, highlights the importance of safeguarding free speech in a climate where misinformation laws are applied inconsistently and arbitrarily. Unfortunately, governments across India are increasingly turning to laws like the Unlawful Activities (Prevention) Act (“ UAPA ”), 1967, and sedition provisions to criminalise misinformation, particularly when it is critical of the state (see here and here ). These laws, originally designed to address genuine threats to national security, have been extended to target online speech, where the broad and often vague definitions of “unlawful activities” and “sedition” can be leveraged to suppress dissent. This trend is troubling, as it risks misusing these provisions to stifle legitimate political discourse, further eroding protections for free expression in the digital era. Conclusion The state-level responses to misinformation reveal key concerns around freedom of speech. The growing role of law enforcement and criminalisation of misinformation, combined with the absence of clear, standardised criteria, raises the risk of arbitrary enforcement and censorship. Without transparent guidelines, legitimate dissent may be wrongly targeted. The lack of uniformity across states—some using police, others cyber units or complaint-based systems—leads to inconsistent treatment of citizens based on geography. This fragmented approach heightens the risk of biased regulation and misuse of power. A more transparent, standardised, and legally grounded framework is urgently needed. Clear definitions, independent oversight, and safeguards must be put in place to ensure efforts to combat misinformation do not compromise democratic freedoms.

articles.title: How Ranveer Allahbadia’s Controversy on ‘India’s Got Latent’ could bring in more stringent OTT regulations
articles.summary: In the Ranveer Allahbadia case, the Supreme Court has signaled its disapproval of the existing digital content and creator ecology. We look closely at the court proceedings that are potentially paving the way for the revival of the shelved Broadcasting Bill.
articles.content: Donate Now tl;dr In the Ranveer Allahbadia case, the Supreme Court has signaled its disapproval of the existing digital content and creator ecology. We look closely at the court proceedings that potentially paving the way for the revival of the shelved Broadcasting Bill. Background In early February 2025, video clips from comedian Samay Raina’s YouTube panel show India’s Got Latent — featuring Ranveer “BeerBiceps” Allahbadia, Ashish Chanchlani, Jaspreet Singh, and Apoorva Mukhija—went viral. Some viewers condemned the jokes, which used explicit sexual references to parents, as vulgar and obscene. The backlash centred on Raina and Allahbadia. On 10 February 2025 the Guwahati Police registered a first‑information report (“FIR”) against the five creators for “promoting obscenity”, citing: (a) Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (“BNS”) Sections 79, 95, 294, 296; (b) Cinematograph Act, 1952 Sections 4, 7; and the (c) Indecent Representation of Women (Prohibition) Act, 1986 Sections 4, 6. The Maharashtra Cyber Department followed suit, booking them under BNS Sections 79, 196, 296, 299 and s. 67 of the Information Technology Act, 2000 at Nodal Cyber Police Station, Thane. Press reports also point to an FIR in Jaipur . Confronted with parallel proceedings in three states, Allahbadia petitioned the Supreme Court (W.P. (Crl.) 85/2025) to quash—or at least consolidate—the Assam and Maharashtra cases. His lawyers mentioned the case before a Bench headed by the Chief Justice of India on 14 February for urgent listing, and the Court scheduled it for hearing on 18 February 2025. Timeline of court proceedings The legal roller‑coaster began on 14 February 2025, when Ranveer Allahbadia hurried to the Supreme Court seeking protection from arrest and a cascade of FIRs registered across the country over an episode of India’s Got Latent . On 18 February 2025, the Bench granted him interim relief but attached four tight conditions: he must cooperate fully with investigators, surrender his passport, remain in India, and halt all new episodes of his show. During the hearing Justice Vikram Nath stated the programme’s language “dirty” and “perverted.” Excerpt from the SC order dated 18.02.2025 in W.P. (Crl.) 83 of 2025 That same afternoon Ashish Chanchlani, another panellist, secured parallel interim protection from the Gauhati High Court; three days later, the Supreme Court formally tagged his case to Allahbadia’s petition. Excerpt from the Guwahati HC order dated 18.02.2025 in Case No. : AB/359/2025 A semblance of reprieve arrived on 3 March. Acknowledging that the podcast supported roughly 280 jobs, the Supreme Court allowed Allahbadia to restart production—provided he kept the conversation “decent” and steered clear of matters under judicial consideration. Crucially, the Bench asked the Solicitor General to draft proposals for a national regime to regulate online content without trampling free‑speech safeguards. This gave rise to fears of the revival of the Broadcasting Bill which IFF has commented on and analysed extensively. Excerpt from the SC order dated 03.03.2025 in W.P. (Crl.) 83 of 2025 Procedural hearings followed. On 7 March the Gauhati High Court extended anticipatory bail to Chanchlani, while Allahbadia, on 1 April, filed an affidavit detailing his cooperation with the National Commission for Women and cyber‑police officials. The Solicitor General reported that investigations in Maharashtra and Assam were nearing completion, so the Court kept interim protections alive. The case soon intersected with disability rights. Excerpt from the Guwahati HC order dated 07.03.2025 in Case No. : AB/359/2025 Excerpt from the SC order dated 01.04.2025 in W.P. (Crl.) 83 of 2025 On 21 April, the State of Maharashtra informed the Court that its investigation was complete and a charge sheet would be filed soon; Assam stated that its investigation was almost complete. Excerpt from the SC order dated 21.04.2025 in W.P. (Crl.) 83 of 2025 Meanwhile, M/s Cure SMA Foundation sought to join the matter, objecting to Samay Raina’s allegedly ableist jokes. The Court agreed to consider the intervention and asked for a full petition, stressing that creative liberty cannot override the dignity of marginalised communities. Subsequently, matters escalated on the hearing on 28 April. While hearing Cure SMA Foundation’s petition, court observed that the issue of content regulation on OTT platforms falls within the domain of policy-making. The petition had raised concerns regarding the alleged circulation of vulgar and inappropriate material on streaming platforms. While the Court declined to frame regulations itself, citing the principle of separation of powers, it directed the petitioner to serve a copy of the petition to the Central Government for its consideration. On the same date of hearing, the Supreme Court allowed Allahbadia to retrieve his passport but refused to club the Maharashtra and Assam FIRs on that date. Justice Surya Kant reasoned that the Assam complaint alleged distinct offences and its residents should not be forced to litigate in Mumbai. The Bench also chastised police for publicly insinuating Allahbadia’s guilt before filing a chargesheet. That day it instructed Cure SMA to obtain details from Mumbai’s Cyber Cell so every relevant respondents could served and be brought on record. Excerpt from the SC order dated 28.04.2025 in W.P. (Crl.) 83 of 2025 Finally, on 5 May, the Supreme Court ordered the Mumbai Police Commissioner to serve notices on Samay Raina and four other creators, warning that coercive measures would follow any no‑show. The judges reiterated their intention to frame comprehensive guidelines for online content—a signal that the spirit of the shelved Broadcasting Bill might soon re‑emerge in regulatory form. These proceedings have given a rise to concern and alarm. Excerpt from the SC order dated 05.05.2025 in W.P. (Crl.) 83 of 2025 The judges reiterated their intention to frame comprehensive guidelines for online content—a signal that the spirit of the shelved Broadcasting Bill might soon re‑emerge in regulatory form. These proceedings have given a rise to concern and alarm. Excerpt from Live Law reporting dated 05.05.2025 in W.P. (Crl.) 83 of 2025 Proactive Move by MIB: Advisory to OTT Platforms on Content Regulation On 20 February 2025, two days after the Supreme Court lamented a “vacuum in law” on digital speech, the Ministry of Information and Broadcasting (“MIB”) issued an advisory telling over‑the‑top (OTT) services to obey existing rules. Citing a spike in complaints from MPs, regulators and parents about “obscene, pornographic and vulgar” videos, the ministry reminded platforms that the IT Rules 2021 Code of Ethics, the Indecent Representation of Women Act, the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita 2023, the POCSO Act and the Information Technology Act 2000 all criminalise unlawful sexual content. It urged stricter age ratings and warned the two self‑regulatory bodies for OTTs to act against violations. While some may state that the advisory adds no new obligations; it merely re‑states laws that already bind streamers. Even so, the timing—just a week after a parliamentary panel decried “vulgar content” online—suggests the government is preparing the ground for tougher, possibly pre‑screening, controls on digital media. Excerpt from the advisory dated 19.02.2025 issued by MIB While the term "regulation" may sound like an innocuous word, it’s important to recall that the State’s previously proposed legal framework - Broadcasting Services (Regulation) Bill, 2023 (“Broadcasting Bill”), which was so draconian that it faced widespread criticism and was ultimately shelved. The MIB released the Broadcasting Bill 2023 for public consultation on November 10, 2023. The draft bill included “Over-the-Top” (“OTT”) content & digital news published by individuals under the regulatory ambit raising concerns for online free speech and journalistic freedom. Subsequently, in July 2024, another version of the Broadcasting Bill was released but never officially published by MIB, however, several unofficial copies of the 2024 Bill were circulated online. The 2024 Bill reportedly imposed compliance obligations on digital creators impacting their online free speech and creative expression, increased the ambit of persons/ entities regulated under the Bill, retained and introduced ambiguous definitions for significant provisions, and negatively impacted users of online platforms. Read more about the Bill here and here . Last year, amid severe pushback and criticism, the MIB reportedly asked stakeholders to return physical copies of the 2024 Broadcasting Bill, promising to reconsider certain provisions. On August 12, 2024, in a post on X, MIB announced that a revised draft would follow consultations, with the comment deadline extended to October 15. Since then, there has been no update. Despite the MIB’s continued opacity, cosmetic changes won’t fix the Bill’s deep flaws. We demand that the MIB #KillTheBill to protect free speech for digital creators, political commentators, users and other key stakeholders. We have been monitoring these developments and will continue to do so closely. Important Documents IFF’s First Read of the Broadcasting Services (Regulation) Bill, 2023 ( Link ) IFF’s summary of the developments around the Broadcasting Bill since July 2024 ( Link ) SC’s March 3, 2025 order directing the Solicitor General to draft proposals on online content regulation ( Link ) Donate Now

articles.title: Section 44(3) and the Systematic Dismantling of the RTI Act: A Fact Check to Ashwini Vaishnaw
articles.summary: Section 3 has no relevance to the RTI amendment, and Mr. Ashwini Vaishnaw's response fails to address the core concern: Section 44(3) weakens citizens’ right to information and transparency in governance. IFF does a fact check.
articles.content: tl;dr On March 26, 2025, over 120 MPs from the INDIA bloc urged the repeal of Section 44(3) of the Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023, warning that it undermines the RTI Act by removing the public interest test in Section 8(1)(j). Shortly after, in response to a letter by Mr. Jairam Ramesh, Mr. Ashwini Vaishnaw cited Section 3 of the DPDPA to claim that the RTI framework remains protected—but this is misleading. Section 3 has no relevance to the RTI amendment, and his response fails to address the core concern: Section 44(3) weakens citizens’ right to information and transparency in governance. IFF does a fact check. Background On March 26, 2025, Over 120 Members of Parliament from the INDIA bloc addressed a joint letter to the Minister for Electronics and Information Technology (“ MeitY ”) and the Union Minister of Information and Broadcasting, Mr. Ashwini Vaishnaw, calling for the repeal of Section 44(3) of the Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023 (“ DPDPA ”). Their appeal reflects a growing political consensus and echoes long-standing civil society concerns—that this provision strikes at the heart of the Right to Information Act, 2005 (“ RTI Act ”)—and must be withdrawn. On April 10, 2025, Mr. Vaishnaw issued a response to a separate letter dated March 23, 2025 by MP Jairam Ramesh, who had warned that Section 44(3) of the DPDPA would hollow out the RTI framework. His concern was precise: that by exempting all “personal information” from disclosure, the amended law would enable public authorities to deny access to vital data—such as details of subsidy disbursal and welfare schemes—simply because it pertains to individuals, whether beneficiaries or officials. The DPDPA passed without meaningful public consultation, introduces a major change to the RTI framework. Section 44 amends Section 8(1)(j) of the RTI Act, and permits authorities to withhold any “personal information” without applying the earlier public interest test. This removes the crucial balance between privacy and transparency. It allows officials to deny access to information even when the data is vital for public accountability. Due to the vague definition of “personal information,” authorities may arbitrarily classify data such as tender documents or corporate submissions as private. The amendment risks turning the DPDPA into a new Official Secrets Act—one that hides governance behind the pretext of privacy. What Mr. Ashwini Vaishnaw Gets Wrong Mr. Vaishnaw’s defence disregards over a decade of expert recommendations and parliamentary deliberation. The Justice A.P. Shah Committee (2012) explicitly cautioned against allowing data protection to override access rights under the RTI Act. The Srikrishna Committee (2018) recommended narrowly tailored exemptions—permitting non-disclosure only where there is a risk of grave harm, such as identity theft, discrimination, or blackmail. Even the Joint Parliamentary Committee reviewing the 2019 version of the Data Protection Bill—comprising members from across party lines—did not recommend any amendment to the RTI Act. Section 3 Cannot Justify Section 44 (3) of the DPDPA’s Attack on RTI It is a fact that Section 44(3) of the DPDPA amends Section 8(1)(j) of RTI Act . This amendment has significantly weakened the RTI framework, as it allows authorities to deny access to information simply by labeling it as “personal information.” This concern has been raised repeatedly and with extensive reasoning. You can read more about the effect of the amendment on RTI here . However, the recent response by Mr. Ashwini Vaishnaw is incorrect on a reading of law and facts. Mr. Vaishnaw in his response dated 10.04.2025 contends that Section 3 of the DPDPA exempts publicly available information and any information that is required to be disclosed by law, suggesting this protects the right to information. However, this position overlooks a critical legal interdependence: the primary law mandating such disclosure is the RTI Act. Specifically, it is the RTI Act that empowers citizens to access information held by public authorities, including certain categories of personal information, unless explicitly exempted under Section 8(1)(j). If this section is amended to remove the public interest requirement that required balancing then public authorities have no option but to provide blanket refusals for any government record that contains, “personal information”. Hence, any public information that contains “personal information” is not made “public” in the first instance itself. Further, the amendment to the RTI Act is not coming from Section 3. Section 44(3) directly amends Section 8(1)(j) of the RTI Act—the part that deals with exemptions for “personal information”. So while Mr. Vaishnaw highlights Section 3 to defend transparency, that section has nothing to do with the amendment to the RTI Act. Secondly, Mr. Vaishnaw’s suggestion that publicly available information does not fall under the purview of the DPDPA ignores a practical reality. If information is genuinely and proactively made public, people would not need to file RTIs to access it. The fact remains that a large portion of “public” information is not readily accessible, and RTI requests are often the only way for citizens to obtain such data. Weakening the RTI Act by amending Section 8(1)(j) directly impacts this process. Therefore, while Mr. Vaishnaw is correct in stating that Section 3 of the DPDPA exempts certain categories of data from its purview, using it as a justification is disingenuous. It does not address the fundamental concern: that Section 44(3) of the DPDPA dilutes the RTI Act and undermines the citizen’s right to information. That is the core issue, and it remains unaddressed. The Constitutional Balance Between Privacy and Transparency The rights to privacy and information are not in conflict; they are complementary guarantees under Part III of the Constitution to rationalise the imbalance in power between the government and us, the citizens of India. While the right to privacy protects individuals from arbitrary intrusion, the right to information empowers us to hold the state accountable. Both are essential to a functioning democracy. This balance is firmly embedded in Indian constitutional jurisprudence. In K.S. Puttaswamy vs. Union of India WP (C) No. 494 of 2012, the Supreme Court affirmed that privacy and transparency must be reconciled within a proportionality framework—ensuring that restrictions on either right are justified, necessary, and the least restrictive means available. This approach was already reflected in Section 8(1)(j) of the RTI Act, which protected personal information but permitted disclosure where a larger public interest warranted it. Courts over time and in a range of cases have upheld this principle: for instance in Surupsingh Hrya Naik vs. State Of Maharashtra, AIR 2007 BOM 121 and in Vijay Prakash vs. Union of India & Ors AIR 2010 DELHI 7 the Delhi High Court and Bombay High Court clarified that privacy must yield when public accountability is at stake. Even in Girish Ramchandra Deshpande vs. Central Information Commissioner & Ors. , 2012 AIR SCW 5865 the Supreme Court prioritised privacy yet observed that necessary information can be disclosed if it is in the public interest. Through this observation the Supreme Court ensured that transparency must not be compromised even if the Court leaned towards protecting individual privacy. Section 44(3) dismantles this equilibrium. By eliminating the public interest test and creating a blanket exemption for all personal information, it overrides carefully evolved jurisprudence and removes a critical safeguard. The result is not improved privacy protection, but unchecked opacity. Statement from Apar Gupta, Advocate & Founder Director, Internet Freedom Foundation “The change to Section 8(1)(J) the RTI Act is not merely a technical amendment; it is an attack on the ability for citizens to hold the government accountable. The RTI Act now has a cold iron statutory bar that will prevent a public information officer from disclosure of any “personal information”. It will advance state impunity rather than individual privacy. The implications are profound. Investigative journalism, public interest litigation, and civic activism—pillars of a vibrant democracy—risk being undermined. The ability to scrutinise public officials, demand clarity on policy decisions, and hold institutions accountable is imperilled. The RTI Act is not an inconvenience to be legislated away. It is a constitutional commitment to the people of India—a means to hold power to account, to question, to demand answers. Hence, the MPs’ letter and the press conference is a welcome step The repeal of Section 44(3) is not merely a legislative correction—it is a democratic imperative along with a broader review of the data protection law that was passed through parliament without any meaningful debate.”. Important Links IFF Joins RTI Campaign: Ensuring Privacy Protections don’t weaken Transparency. Mr. Jairam Ramesh’s letter to Mr. Ashwini Vaishnaw dated 23.03.2025 . Joint letter by 120 MPs to MeitY Mr. Ashwini Vaishnaw’s response dated 10.04.2025 .

articles.title: Budget Session 2025: A Digital Rights Review
articles.summary: The Budget Session of Parliament, held from January 21 to April 4, 2025, included a recess from February 13 to March 10 for Standing Committee reviews. Key discussions covered various national issues, including digital rights and freedoms.
articles.content: tl;dr The Budget Session of Parliament concluded on April 4, 2025, when both Houses of Parliament adjourned sine die. The session commenced on January 21, 2025 with a recess between February 13, 2025 and March 10, 2025 to enable Department related Standing Committees to examine and report on the Demands for Grants relating to various Ministries/Departments. Discussions on the floor of the parliament spanned several issues, including those relevant to digital rights and freedoms. Background The Budget Session of Parliament commenced on January 21, 2025, and concluded on April 4, 2025, after 26 sittings in the Lok Sabha and the Rajya Sabha over 26 days. During this Session a total of 11 Bills (10 in Lok Sabha and 1 in Rajya Sabha) were introduced. 16 Bills were passed by Lok Sabha and 14 Bills were passed/returned by Rajya Sabha with the total number of Bills passed by both Houses of Parliament being 16. The session’s productivity was quite high, with the Lok Sabha operating at 111% and the Rajya Sabha at 112% of their scheduled time. Question Hour functioned for 78% and 83% of its scheduled time in Lok Sabha and Rajya Sabha respectively. However, only eleven Bills were passed in the first year of 18th Lok Sabha, marking it as the lowest output since 1999. This highlights the continuing challenge of legislative backlogs with 28 Bills remaining pending before the Parliament. Quick Overview: Union Budget The Union Budget for 2025-26 reflects a significant decline in overall allocations for Department of Telecommunications and Ministry of Home Affairs as compared to 2024-25 and meagre increment to the Ministry of Information and Broadcasting while Ministry of Electronics and Technology saw 18.64% increased allocations partly due to the prioritization of India AI mission. The total budgetary allocation for the Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology (“ MeitY ”) in the Union Budget 2025-26 stood at ₹26,026.25 crore, reflecting an 18.64% increase over the Budget Estimates of 2024-25. Even as the MeitY’s estimated expenditure increased, the total Budget of Telecom and IT sector in general came down by 0.65% points from last year (2.41% in FY25 to 1.88% in FY26). With the increased utilisation of technology in governance, ballooning of emerging technologies and digitisation being ubiquitous in every walk of life demands more than allotted budget for it to be equitable and accessible to every stratum of society. With AI taking center stage in public discourse and its inevitable growth in the foreseeable future, the India AI Mission aims to bring artificial intelligence (“ AI ”) to critical sectors of the economy. It has been allocated ₹2000 crore, a whopping 1056% increase from the revised estimates in the 2024-25 budget, where ₹173 crore was allotted. This considerable increase comes in the background of a. Technical Secretariat being proposed and other regulatory frameworks being framed to govern AI in the country which is positioned to have a profound socio-technical impact. The Data Protection Board (“ DPB ”) which is the key regulatory authority under the Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023, responsible for enforcing data protection regulations, again saw a mere allocation of Rs. 5 cr. with Rs. 50 lakhs forming the capital outlay which includes fixed assets such as digital portal, and the rest of Rs. 4.5 crores towards revenue expenditure. A sum of ₹574.8 crore is allocated for Census Survey and Statistics/Registrar General of India this year compared to ₹1,309.46 crore in the July 2024 Budget, giving a clear indication that the decadal exercise will be further delayed. Further, the continued underfunding of digital literacy programs like PMGDISHA remains a glaring issue, as increased investments in technology without inclusive accessibility measures could widen the digital divide. Additionally, the historical underutilization of funds in telecom and cybersecurity sectors raises concerns about whether increased allocations will result in effective implementation. You can read more about it here . Media Council for better implementation The Parliamentary Standing Committee on Communications and Information Technology recommended the constitution of a media council for various forms of media, including print, broadcasting, and digital, to ensure better implementation of laws governing these services. This came as part of the Standing Committee’s report on the demand for grants for 2025-26, presented before the Lok Sabha. In this report, the Committee noted that the Ministry of Information and Broadcasting (“ MIB ”) has been actively integrating AI into the media sector through initiatives such as SAMVAD, an AI-powered dashboard that monitors social media trends, and AI news anchors on DD Kisan, which deliver agricultural news 24/7 in multiple languages. The Committee commended these efforts and further encouraged expanding AI adoption in content creation, misinformation monitoring, and administrative efficiency. While the push for integrating AI aims to improve efficiency and tackle misinformation, it raises several concerns. AI systems operate on a large scale, monitoring real-time trends across social media, news, and broadcasting with minimal human oversight, increasing the risk of over-censorship and privacy violations. SAMVAD, with its ability to track social media trends, sentiment, and feedback, could potentially be misused for surveillance. Read more about it here . Dilution of right to information The amendment to the Right to Information (“ RTI ”) Act, 2005, through Section 44(3) of the Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023, has sparked significant concerns regarding its potential impact on citizens' ability to access crucial information. By exempting all personal information from disclosure, the amendment removes the carefully balanced provisions of the RTI Act that allowed personal data to be withheld only if it had no public interest or would result in an unwarranted invasion of privacy. This blanket exemption threatens to deny access to information essential for public accountability, such as names of willful defaulters, contractors involved in government projects, and lists of individuals benefiting from public schemes. In response to these concerns, a delegation of civil society members, including IFF, and other activists, academics, researchers, and journalists, met with Lok Sabha Leader of Opposition Rahul Gandhi and MPs from several political parties on March 25. The delegation, which included prominent figures such as Nikhil Dey, Anjali Bhardwaj, Apar Gupta, and Jayati Ghosh, raised alarms over the chilling effect the DPDP Act could have on journalists, activists, unions, and political parties, limiting their ability to access and use vital information. Several MPs, including Digvijay Singh (INC), Manoj Kumar Jha (RJD), Mahua Moitra (TMC), and others, endorsed a letter to Minister Ashwini Vaishnaw, urging the repeal of the amendments made to the RTI Act, which they argued would severely hinder transparency and accountability. Digital rights in the parliament Throughout every Session of the Parliament, we track significant answers to parliamentary questions placed in both Houses by various members. Starred and unstarred questions submitted by MPs are a great tool for holding the incumbent government accountable. Answered by the Union Ministers, the responses become an invaluable source of authentic and legitimate information. AI in India AI tools in Agriculture: Lok Sabha (“ LS ”) MP Mahua Moitra raised questions to the Minister of Agriculture and Farmers Welfare about the deployment of AI in agriculture, including the outcomes of any such implementation. The Minister of State for Agriculture and Farmers Welfare summarised the initiatives taken by the government including the AI powered chatbot ‘Kisan e-Mitra’ and the national pest surveillance system which utilizes AI and machine learning to detect pest infestation in crop issues, enabling timely intervention for healthier crops. However, concerns remain about the security of sensitive data processed by these applications, highlighting the urgent need for robust security frameworks and strict usage guidelines to mitigate risks. Job displacement due to AI: Rajya Sabha (“ RS ”) MP AA Rahim inquired on impact studies conducted by the government to assess the effects of AI on job displacement across various sectors as well as the steps taken by Government to prevent or mitigate job losses in the private sector caused by the increasing adoption of AI. Further, he asked for information regarding any proposed regulatory framework to monitor and regulate the use of AI to ensure that it does not worsen the unemployment crisis. Minister of State for Labour and Employment Sushri Shobha Karandlaje responded that deployment of AI may result in job creation in various streams like data science and curation, adding that, so far 8.65 Lakh candidates have enrolled/trained in various courses including 3.20 lakh candidates in AI/big data analytics technologies. AI in the Pharmaceutical Sector: RS MPs GC Chandrashekhar and Ashokrao Shankarrao Chavan inquired on any proposals taken by the government on the deployment of AI in the pharmaceutical industry. The Minister of State for the Ministry of Chemicals and Fertilizers, Anupriya Patel, responded that the Department of Pharmaceuticals has taken steps to promote research and development in the sector through the Scheme for Promotion of Research and Innovation in the Pharma Medtech sector. Further, the Pharmaceuticals and Medical Devices Bureau of India under the Department of Pharmaceuticals, with the assistance of the Centre for Development of Advanced Computing, has undertaken a pilot project to evaluate the feasibility of a block-chain-based track-and-trace system for Pradhan Mantri Bhartiya Janaushadhi Pariyojana. AI and health : LS MP Radheshyam Rathiya asked about the deployment of AI in the health sector and whether any comprehensive assessment has been conducted to understand the fields where AI can be deployed effectively. The Minister of State for the Ministry of Health and Family Welfare, Prataprao Jadhav, responded that the the Ministry of Health and Family Welfare is leveraging AI in public health services across India. As part of this, the Ministry of Health has designated AIIMS Delhi, PGIMER Chandigarh and AIIMS Rishikesh as ‘Centre of Excellence for Artificial Intelligence’ with an aim to promote development and use of AI based solutions in Health. He added that this includes ‘Media Disease Surveillance’, which is an AI-driven tool that has been supporting event-based surveillance for infectious diseases since April 2022. This tool scans digital news sources across the country and shares relevant information with districts for early action and response. According to the Minister, since April 2022, Media Disease Surveillance has published over 4,500 event alerts, contributing to the timely prevention and mitigation of disease outbreaks, thereby reducing mortality and morbidity. However, such deployment without any safeguards raises concerns about transparency and accountability, specifically relating to information regarding the personal health data of individuals. The opacity of AI algorithms makes decisions harder to challenge or appeal, while the vast data collection required for trend analysis could lead to misuse or a chilling effect on free speech. Though AI might seem more objective if implemented by private or academic partnerships, its widespread automation and the potential to systematically shape narratives without transparency make it a potentially greater threat to information freedom. Drones for Surveillance RS MP Sanjeev Arora asked the Minister of Civil Aviation on the total number of commercial drones registered in the country as of September 2024, categorized based on the purpose. In addition, he inquired about the specific measures that exist to promote the use of drones in sectors like agriculture, healthcare, and infrastructure and other pertinent details. Drones equipped with surveillance technology (such as cameras and sensors) can potentially capture footage of individuals, residential areas, or restricted zones without consent, leading to concerns about unauthorized surveillance. Read our thread summarizing the response here . PM Kisan and technological inclusivity LS MP Arun Bharti asked the Minister of Agriculture and Farmers Welfare about the PM-Kisan scheme's use of technology to streamline beneficiary identification, ensure transparency in fund disbursal, and integrate with other welfare schemes. The Minister of State for Agriculture and Farmers Welfare Ramnath Thakur informed that the PM-Kisan scheme transfers ₹6,000 annually to land-holding farmers via Direct Benefit Transfer (DBT) using Aadhaar-seeded bank accounts, ensuring direct & timely payments. The scheme relies on Aadhaar, Income Tax, and PFMS cross-checks for eligibility, with physical verification for accuracy. However, this tech-driven process risks excluding farmers without Aadhaar, bank accounts, or formal land records, particularly those in remote areas. While manual checks help, they might not catch all issues, and rural areas may still face gaps in verification. Read our thread summarising the response here . On E-Shram Portal RS MP Neeraj Dangi asked the Minister of Labour and Employment about the Aadhaar verification system in the e-Shram portal, and whether workers have been deprived of welfare benefits due to technical glitches. The Minister of Labour and Employment launched e-Shram portal on August 26, 2021 for creation of a comprehensive National Database of Unorganised Workers seeded with Aadhaar. The e-Shram portal is meant to register and support the unorganised workers by providing them a Universal Account Number (UAN) on a self-declaration basis. Similarly, RS MP Dharmasthala Veerendra Heggade inquired on the actions taken by the government to ensure implementation of various labour codes at e- commerce platforms and whether the government proposes to frame suitable social security schemes for gig workers, platform workers as well as workers in the unorganized sector on matters relating to life, disability cover, accident insurance, health and maternity benefits and old age protection. The Minister for Labour and Employment merely pointed out that the government, in its budget announcement had proposed to arrange for their identity cards and registration on the e-Shram portal and provide health care under AB-PMJAY health scheme without expanding on the actions taken to improve implementation of labour safeguards. Read our thread summarising the response here . To know more, read our explainer on the E-Shram portal here . Fake News? RS MP Abdul Wahab questioned the Minister of Information and Broadcasting about the Government's measures to combat fake news and disinformation, and asked whether any action has been taken against TV channels for spreading it. The Minister of State of the Ministry of Information and Broadcasting, Dr. L Murugan, pointed out that newspapers have to adhere to “Norms of Journalistic Conduct” brought out by Press Council of India (“ PCI ”) which, inter alia, restrains publication of fake/ defamatory/ misleading news. The Council holds inquiry into alleged violations of Norms, and may warn, admonish or censure the newspaper, editors, journalists, etc. as the case may be. Read our thread summarising the response here . Ethical Considerations in Neurotechnology RS MP Dr. D Ravi Kumar asked the Ministry of Health and Family Welfare about the steps taken by the government to recognize and protect neurorights, referencing Chile’s constitutional amendment, which ensures that technological developments respect individuals’ physical and mental integrity. In addition, he inquired whether the Government is considering Colorado's recent law to protect individuals’ neurological privacy and California's deliberations on a similar instrument and the actions taken/being taken by the Government to collaborate with inter-governmental organizations working on the human rights dimension of neurotechnologies to ensure the protection of citizens’ rights. The Minister of State for Ministry of Health and Family Welfare Prataprao Jadhav stated that India currently lacks specific laws on neuro rights but cited existing frameworks like the IT Act, the Digital Personal Data Protection Act, and the Mental Healthcare Act for data protection and privacy. This raises concerns as neurotechnologies evolve, the absence of legal safeguards presents issues about mental privacy and cognitive surveillance. Without clear protections, individuals may be vulnerable to intrusive data collection and manipulation. Read our thread summarising the response here . One Too Many Fact-Check Units LS MP G Kumar Naik asked for details of instances where fact-checking led to social media content takedowns. Further, they inquired whether an independent, non-partisan fact-checking body is being considered by the Government. The Minister of State for Ministry of Electronics and Information Broadcasting, Ashwini Vaishnaw, provided details about the queries received and resolved during the last three years and the current year by the Fact Check Unit (“ FCU ”) set up under the PIB in November. He also highlighted a four-step model for verification of the reported news/content by the FCU. This includes: 1. Find: The PIB Fact Check Unit takes suo-moto cognizance on fact checking as well as receives complaints on its website or WhatsApp Hotline, 2. Assess: The information is then segregated after ascertaining whether it falls within the ambit of the FCU, 3. Create: The FCU utilizes then creates awareness on social media platforms; and 4. Target: FCU posts the correct information on its social media handles. The lack of procedural safeguards in the PIB's FCU raises concerns about transparency and accountability as it functions with suo-motu cognizance and content dissemination without clear oversight. In September 2024, the Bombay High Court ruling held the establishment of FCUs under the IT (Amendment) Rules, 2023, as unconstitutional. This raises questions about the government’s disregard for judicial rulings. Read our thread summarising the response here . On Internet Shutdowns LS MPs Saptagiri Sankar Ulaka, Madhavaneni Raghunandan Rao, Jugal Kishore, Jagdambika Pal and Anurag Sharma asked about the the number and the details of internet shutdown orders issued under the Temporary Suspension of Telecom Services (Public Emergency or Public Safety) Rules, 2017 between January 2023 and January 2025, and inquired whether any economic and/or social impact assessment of internet shutdowns in the country has been conducted. In response, the Ministry of Communications acknowledged that no study has been conducted on the effectiveness of shutdowns, despite the Supreme Court’s recognition of their constitutional implications on rights like free speech and online professions. The recently notified Telecommunications (Temporary Suspension of Services) Rules, 2024, mandate that shutdown orders include specific reasons, geographical areas, and durations while maintaining provisions for the publication of these orders. However, the rules do not specify the exact method or mechanism of publication. The Review Committee’s power to set aside suspension orders under the draft rules has also been retained in the notified rules. (Read more about the recently notified Suspension Rules, 2024, here ). A similar question was asked by LS MP Sukhdeo Bhagat who pointed out that the country has recorded 84 internet shutdowns, the highest among democratic nations, with 21 occurring in Manipur alone in 2024. He inquired on the reasons for not undertaking comprehensive evaluations of the consequences of internet shutdowns by the Government given the significant implications for access to information, economic activities and digital rights and any plans regarding the implementation of an oversight mechanism to prevent arbitrary suspensions in the future. The Minister of State for Ministry of Communications was evasive in their response, answering that the “contribution of internet for the well-being of citizens has to be balanced with the misuse of social media platforms by anti-social elements which may require temporary internet shutdowns”. Conclusion The Budget Session ended with heated protests and discussions. On the digital rights front, while this session saw increased engagement on the issues of digitalisation of PDS and AI governance, the government’s responses often revealed gaps between ambitious digital policies and their implementation. The handling of critical matters like PM Kisan and disinformation suggests a concerning trend toward reduced transparency in digital governance. Perhaps most telling is the government’s acknowledgement that no studies have been conducted on the effectiveness of internet shutdowns, despite their frequent use and significant impact on digital rights.

articles.title: Parliamentary Standing Committee pushes for AI in Broadcasting and a Unified Media Regulation
articles.summary: On March 21, 2025, a parliamentary panel urged AI to curb misinformation and sought stakeholder input on the proposed Broadcasting Services (Regulation) Bill in its report on I&B Ministry grants.
articles.content: tl;dr On March 21, 2025, the Parliamentary Standing Committee on Communications and Information Technology presented its 11th Report on the Demands for Grants (2025-2026) of the Ministry of Information and Broadcasting to the Lok Sabha.
